chunk_summary: India faces a high burden of wasting in children under 5, with 19.3% wasted and 7.7% severely wasted. Clinically actionable determinants include diet quality, feeding practices, infections, and WASH, requiring integrated prevention and treatment strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the prevalence and clinical significance of moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in India.
Identify key dietary and health-related drivers of wasting in children under 5.
Learn to develop integrated treatment plans addressing both nutrition and illness in MAM cases.
Recognize the bidirectional relationship between infection and undernutrition.
key_facts: 19.3% of children under 5 in India are wasted, with 7.7% severely wasted (NFHS-5, 2019–2021).
Moderate wasting is estimated at 11.6% by subtracting severe wasting from total wasting.
Key drivers include inadequate diet, inappropriate feeding practices, recurrent infections, and suboptimal WASH.
Wasting is sensitive to short-term shocks like food insecurity and seasonal infection peaks.
Effective outpatient care for MAM can prevent progression to severe wasting and reduce avoidable deaths.
reference_quote: The NFHS-5 (2019–2021) fact sheet reports 19.3% of under-5 children are wasted and 7.7% are severely wasted.
document_key_topics: Integrated prevention and treatment strategies for wasting
Impact of infections and WASH on child nutrition
